1: 'So these three men ceased to answer Job, because he was righteous in his own eyes.' The notice that the three friends 'ceased to answer' (wayyišbᵊṯû, came to rest, stopped) because Job was 'righteous in his own eyes' (ṣaddîq bᵊʿênāyw) signals the exhaustion of the conventional wisdom dialogue. Job's claim to innocence (ṣaddîq) in his own sight is not simply pride — it is the forensic assertion of the oath in Job 31. The friends have been unable to answer, not because they are convinced but because they have run out of arguments. The stage is now set for a new voice.
2: 'Then Elihu the son of Barachel the Buzite, of the family of Ram, burned with anger.' The introduction of Elihu is unique in the dialogue: he is given a complete genealogy (ben-Baraḵʾēl habbûzî mimmišpaḥat Rām), unlike the three friends who were simply identified by their cities (Eliphaz the Temanite, Bildad the Shuhite, Zophar the Naamathite). The Buzite identification connects Elihu to the region near Teman — Buz appears in Genesis 22:21 as a son of Nahor (Abraham's brother), placing Elihu in the broader Aramaean/patriarchal genealogical world. The complete genealogy signals that Elihu is a real person, not a type, and that his authority claim is grounded in legitimate lineage.
3: 'He burned with anger also at Job's three friends because they had found no answer, although they had declared Job to be in the wrong.' Elihu's anger at the three friends has a different basis from his anger at Job: the friends have condemned Job without being able to refute his arguments. In the ANE disputation context, this was the worst possible outcome — declaring the opponent wrong without being able to demonstrate it was intellectual dishonesty dressed as theological authority. Elihu's critique anticipates YHWH's verdict (Job 42:7: 'you have not spoken of me what is right, as my servant Job has') — the friends' conclusion was wrong, and their reasoning was insufficient.
4: 'Now Elihu had waited to speak to Job because they were older than he.' The social protocol of deference to elders (ḥikˑkāh ʾeṯ-ʾiyyôḇ biddᵊḇārîm kî zᵊqenîm hēmmāh mimmennû) is the specific cultural context for Elihu's delayed entry into the dialogue. In the ANE wisdom tradition, the young were expected to listen while the elders spoke — the entire wisdom educational system was built on the transmission of knowledge from elder to younger. Sir Job 32:7-9 reflects this same protocol in the Second Temple period: 'speak, young man, only if necessary, and only twice, and then only when pressed; give a brief answer... do not interrupt.' Elihu's waiting is culturally appropriate; his eventual speaking requires justification.
5: 'And when Elihu saw that there was no answer in the mouth of the three men, he burned with anger.' The trigger for Elihu's entry is the exhaustion of the elder disputants: the protocol of deference can be set aside when the elders have demonstrably failed. In the wisdom tradition, the failure of the elders to provide adequate counsel was the recognized occasion for a junior to speak (the Ahiqar tradition, popular throughout the ANE, features a young man whose wisdom surpasses that of the established sages). Elihu's anger is presented as righteous indignation at the theological failure of those who should have answered Job adequately.
6: 'And Elihu the son of Barachel the Buzite answered and said: I am young in years, and you are aged; therefore I was timid and afraid to declare my opinion to you.' Elihu's opening self-identification as young (ṣāʿîr leyāmîm, young in years) and his explicit acknowledgment of timidity (zaḥaltî waʾîrāʾ, I was timid and afraid) before the aged (yīšišîm, the elders) demonstrates that he understands and has been observing the protocol. His deference is not mere politeness but genuine cultural formation in the wisdom tradition. The contrast with the three friends is pointed: they spoke with confident authority; Elihu speaks with acknowledged limitation but ultimately more theological insight.
7: 'I said, "Let days speak, and many years teach wisdom."' The proverbial wisdom that days (yāmîm, years of experience) and many years (rōḇ šānîm) teach wisdom (ḥokmāh) is the standard defense of the elder-authority system. This is the same tradition that Job mockingly cited in Job 12:12 ('wisdom is with the aged') — but Elihu cites it as a sincere belief that he has actually followed by waiting for the elders to exhaust their speeches. The difference between Elihu and Job is that Elihu has genuinely subordinated himself to the social protocol; Job's citation in Job 12 was sarcastic dismissal of the friends' false claim to wisdom.
8: 'But it is the spirit in man, the breath of the Almighty, that makes him understand.' The theological justification for Elihu's speaking despite his youth appeals to the divine spirit (rûaḥ... wənišmaṯ šadday, the spirit... and the breath of the Almighty) as the source of understanding. This is the same spirit that animated the human being at creation (Genesis 2:7: nišmaṯ ḥayyîm, the breath of life). Elihu's argument is pneumatological: the spirit of God, not the accumulation of years, is the ultimate source of wisdom. This theological move positions him to speak without the accumulated years — if the spirit has given understanding, age is a secondary qualification, not a primary one.
9: 'It is not the old who are wise, nor the elders who understand what is right.' The explicit challenge to elder-authority is a remarkable social move in the ANE context. Elihu is directly contradicting the foundational assumption of the wisdom educational system: that age is equivalent to wisdom. In the Israelite prophetic tradition, there was a recognized tension between the wisdom of the elders and the charismatic authority of the spirit-inspired prophet (cf. the young Jeremiah's claim against the established temple authorities). Elihu's position combines the wisdom tradition's pneumatological claim with the prophetic tradition's challenge to institutional authority.
10: 'Therefore I say, "Listen to me; let me also declare my opinion."' The formal claim to speak (šimᵊʿû-lî ʾăḥawweh daʿ-ʾap̄ gam-ʾānî) mirrors Job's own demand for a hearing in Job 13. The wisdom disputation's formal protocol required each speaker to establish their right to speak before making their argument. Elihu's establishment of the right to speak (despite his youth) through the theological argument of Job 32:8-9 positions him as a legitimate new voice in the dialogue — one who has waited respectfully but now speaks by divine authorization.
11: 'Behold, I waited for your words, I listened for your wise sayings, while you searched out what to say.' The description of waiting (ḥikˑkîṯî ʾet-ḏiḇrêkem) and careful listening for wise sayings (ṯûšiyyōṯêkem, your wise practical maxims — the same tûšiyyāh term used in 12:16 for divine wisdom) while they 'searched out what to say' frames the three friends' speeches as effortful searches rather than fluent wisdom. The image of searching for words implies difficulty — the best they could manage required effort. In the oral culture's standards for wisdom speech, the truly wise man spoke with ease because wisdom was already in him; the effortful speech betrayed its inadequacy.
12: 'I gave you my full attention, and behold, there was none among you who refuted Job or who answered his words.' The devastating verdict: after all the speeches, not one of the three friends has successfully refuted (môkîaḥ, one who reproves/refutes) Job. Môkîaḥ is the technical term for the one who successfully demonstrates the opponent's error in the disputation — Eliphaz had promised this role (Job 22:4) but failed to deliver it. Elihu's assessment is that the entire dialogue has been a failure of refutation: the friends talked extensively but did not answer the substance of Job's case.
13: 'Beware of saying, "We have found wisdom; God may vanquish him, not a man."' Elihu warns against the friends' temptation to claim that they have found wisdom while conceding that only God can actually answer Job. This is the intellectual dishonesty of the position: to declare that Job is in the wrong but then retreat to 'God will have to deal with him' is to admit that human argument has failed. In the honor context of the wisdom disputation, this retreat would be a face-saving maneuver that preserves the claim to wisdom while avoiding the actual task of answering. Elihu refuses this maneuver and positions himself as the one who will actually answer Job on the human plane before the divine answer comes.
14: 'He has not directed his words against me, and I will not answer him with your speeches.' Elihu's declaration of methodological independence is significant: Job's words have not been directed against Elihu personally, so he has no personal stake in refuting them — he can approach the matter without the defensiveness of the three friends, who had been personally challenged by Job's accusations. Furthermore, he will not use their speeches (bᵊmillekem): he is not simply repeating their arguments but bringing something new. The methodological novelty is the basis for Elihu's claim to speak — he has a different approach, not merely a rehearsal of the failed arguments.
15: 'They are dismayed; they answer no more; they have not a word to say.' The description of the three friends as dismayed (ḥaṯˑtû, terrified, shattered — the same root as ḥittāh, the terror that the friends warned would fall on Job if he continued his challenge in Job 13:11) is ironic: the terror they predicted for Job has actually fallen on them. They 'answer no more' — their speechlessness is the formal proof of their defeat in the disputation. In the ancient oral culture, the loss of speech was the loss of the argument; the man who cannot speak has conceded the contest.
16: 'And shall I wait, because they do not speak, because they stand there, and answer no more?' The rhetorical question formally justifies Elihu's breaking of the waiting protocol: since the elders have fallen silent (ʿāmᵊdû lōʾ-ʿānû-ʿôḏ hēʾādîqû mimmennî millîm, they stood there and answered no more), the social reason for his silence no longer exists. The waiting was for the elders to exhaust their speeches; that exhaustion has now occurred. The protocol's conditions having been met, Elihu is now legitimately free to speak without violating the social order — his speech is not an interruption but a succession.
17: 'I also will answer with my share; I also will declare my opinion.' The phrase 'my share' (ḥelqî, my portion, my assigned part) treats the disputation as a communal endeavor in which each participant has an assigned share. In the wisdom education context, the dialogue had specific roles: the master asked, the student answered, and the disputation proceeded in assigned turns. Elihu's 'share' is the contribution of the participant who has been waiting his turn. The word daʿat (opinion, knowledge, understanding) is the same vocabulary as the divine wisdom that Elihu claimed in Job 32:8 — he will speak from that divinely-given understanding.
18: 'For I am full of words; the spirit within me constrains me.' The fullness of words (mālēʾṯî millîm) and the constraining spirit (rûaḥ biṭˑnî yᵊṣêqannî) describe the prophetic experience of compelled speech — the inability not to speak when the divine word is pressing for expression. This language resonates with the prophetic tradition: Jeremiah's 'there is in my heart as it were a burning fire shut up in my bones, and I am weary with holding it in, and I cannot' (Jeremiah 20:9); Amos's 'the lion has roared; who will not fear? The Lord God has spoken; who can but prophesy?' (Amos 3:8). Elihu's speech is presented as quasi-prophetic compulsion, not merely intellectual confidence.
19: 'Behold, my belly is like wine that has no vent; like new wineskins ready to burst.' The wine-in-a-sealed-vessel image is familiar from the NT (Matthew 9:17: new wine in old wineskins). In the ancient world, wine that was sealed in a new skin before fermentation was complete would build pressure and burst the skin if not vented. The image captures the physiological pressure of unspoken words in a culture where speech was the primary medium of social existence: the person who must speak but cannot is experiencing genuine somatic distress. The new wine/new wineskin image also suggests that Elihu's contribution will be genuinely new, not merely a recycling of the old arguments.
20: 'I must speak, that I may find relief; I must open my lips and answer.' The necessity of speech (ʾăḏabbᵊrāh wəyirwah-lî, I must speak that I may find relief/breathing-room) is physiological: the constriction of the unexpressed word creates physical distress. This is the ancient world's somatic understanding of emotional and intellectual pressure — the body's experience of the unexpressed is real discomfort. The 'relief' sought is not emotional catharsis in the modern sense but the physical relief of pressure that builds when the mouth is constrained from its natural function.
21: 'I will not show partiality to any man or use flattery toward any person.' Elihu's formal disclaimer of partiality (ʾiśśāʾ-nāʾ p̄ənê-ʾîš, lifting up the face of a man) and flattery (ʾăḵanneh lᵊʾāḏām, giving someone a flattering title) echoes the judicial standard of impartiality that Job appealed to in Job 13:8-10 against the friends (who showed partiality toward God in the disputation by refusing to evaluate the arguments fairly). Elihu positions himself as the honest evaluator that the friends failed to be: he will evaluate both Job's and God's positions impartially, without the social deference that corrupted the friends' arguments.
22: 'For I do not know how to flatter, for then my Maker would soon take me away.' The grounding of non-flattery in the fear of the Maker (ʿōśenî, the one who made me) continues Elihu's theological self-positioning: he speaks truthfully because the God who made him will judge him for flattery. This is the same divine accountability that grounded Job's oath in Job 31:14, 23 — the fear of divine examination as the motivation for honest dealing. Elihu's final self-presentation positions him as a man of divine accountability parallel to Job, but with the claim that his accountability produces a different conclusion about Job's case.
